subject: English
chapter_title: A Friend Found in Music (Twin Melodies)
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 1 periods = 40 minutes
  Row 2: 45 minutes × 2 periods = 90 minutes
  Row 3: 60 minutes × 1 periods = 60 minutes
Total: 4 periods · 3h 10min

periods.activity_title: Poem Reading and Appreciation — Metaphor, Mood, and Rhyme

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Reflect and Respond (p.194): share favourite music, musician, and reasons with class
Reading for Appreciation (p.195): seven appreciation questions — metaphor, simile, rhyme scheme, anaphora, message, mood, speaker evidence

phases.description: Warm-up: Reflect and Respond (p.194) — students briefly share their preferred music type, favourite musician, and reasons. Teacher links responses to the poem's theme of music as emotional companion.

Shared reading: teacher reads the poem aloud once, modelling the anaphoric rhythm of 'Music is…'; students follow. Second read: students read aloud together. Teacher draws attention to the ABCB rhyme scheme and the incantatory effect of repetition.

Reading for Appreciation (p.195): students work through all seven questions — ambiguity of 'moves me', metaphor-to-simile conversion, rhyme scheme impact, purpose of repetition, poet's message, mood identification, and evidence that speaker is the poet. Pairs discuss before sharing with class.
Brief class share: two pairs read their metaphor-to-simile conversions aloud. Teacher previews the extract-based questions for the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem's dominant device is anaphora — 'Music is…' repeated across all three stanzas — which creates the meditative tone described in the poem appreciation summary; ensure students identify this as the source of both the rhythmic effect and the poet's voice. The Reflect and Respond warm-up questions do not have question-bank sub-items and should be kept conversational and brief.

periods.activity_title: Extract Analysis and Extended Response — Music as Universal Companion

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Check Your Understanding (p.195): complete poet's diary — fill blanks with words from poem
Critical Reflection (p.196): five extract-based questions on the therapy stanza
Critical Reflection (p.196): five extended-answer questions on poet's attitude and universal appeal

phases.description: Check Your Understanding (p.195): students complete the six-blank poet's diary entry using words from the poem. Teacher reviews answers quickly to confirm word choice accuracy — this also deepens familiarity with the poem's lexis before analytical work.

Extract critical reflection (p.196): students read the therapy stanza extract and answer all five sub-questions individually — meaning of 'feel blue', line showing happiness, significance of the last line, true-false statement, and central idea selection. Class discusses; teacher draws attention to how a single stanza sustains multiple analytical entry points.

Extended response questions (p.196): students write answers to all five questions on music moving the poet 'to the core', the 'needed friend' comparison, the poet's attitude, universal appeal, and personal comparison. Teacher prompts students to ground responses in specific lines.
One student shares the universal-appeal response; class adds evidence. Teacher notes transition to vocabulary and writing for the poem in the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem's central theme — music as emotional sustenance in both joy and loneliness — is stated directly in the third stanza; encourage students to quote from all three stanzas in their extended responses rather than relying on the therapy stanza alone. The universal-appeal question is the highest-order item; prompt reluctant students by asking them to think of a specific moment when music helped them personally.

periods.activity_title: Emotion Vocabulary, Phrasal Verbs with 'Move', and Word-Building

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Vocabulary in Context (p.197): classify emotion words; choose correct vocabulary in four music-context sentences
Vocabulary in Context (p.198): match six 'move' phrasal verbs to their meanings

homework.task_brief: Vocabulary in Context (p.198): create phrasal verbs from 'put', 'get', 'look', 'break'; write meanings and sentences

phases.description: Emotion vocabulary task (p.197): students classify 'feel blue', 'lifts my spirits', 'most cheerful' into positive and negative emotions, then choose the correct nuanced vocabulary word (nostalgia, anguish, melancholy, jubilant) for each of the four music-context sentences. Teacher highlights that the poem uses simple everyday phrases while the exercise introduces more formal emotional vocabulary.

Phrasal verbs with 'move' (p.198): teacher explains the 'pull through' example from the poem, then students individually match six 'move' phrasal verbs (move out, move in, move on, move over, move along, move off) to their meanings. Class confirms answers; teacher elicits one student-created sentence per phrasal verb.

Consolidation: teacher briefly introduces the word-building task with 'put', 'get', 'look', 'break' and demonstrates one phrasal verb each to scaffold homework. Students are asked to create at least two phrasal verbs per base verb.
Homework set: create phrasal verbs from 'put', 'get', 'look', 'break'; find meanings; write sentences; discuss with classmates next period.

periods.teacher_notes: Students often confuse 'move out' and 'move on' — use a brief board example contrasting physical movement (move out of a house) and figurative progress (move on from a difficulty) to distinguish them. The phrasal-verb creation task for homework is open-ended; students who need a scaffold can use a dictionary's phrasal-verb index.

periods.activity_title: Santoor Listening, Role-Play on Music's Value, and Invitation Letter

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Listen and Respond (p.199): pre-listening prediction — six multiple-choice questions before hearing the conversation
Speaking Activity (p.200): role-play in groups of four — music teacher, two students, parent debating value of music classes

homework.task_brief: Writing Task (p.201): draft invitation letter to grandparents for World Music Day sitar recital on 21 June

phases.description: Listening: pre-listening prediction (p.199) — students read all six multiple-choice questions and guess answers before hearing the conversation. Teacher plays or reads the transcript (p.270, Unit 6); students check and correct predictions. Class discusses answers with reference to the conversation details about the santoor's twice-weekly class limitation and the mother's solution.

Role-play (p.200): groups of four take roles — music teacher, Student 1 (doubtful), Student 2 (convinced), parent (initially doubtful). Groups plan using the cue points, then perform. Teacher notes how the role-play mirrors the play's theme of intergenerational doubt and eventual acceptance of music's value.

Writing task introduction (p.201): teacher reads the invitation letter task aloud, explains the format (sender's address, date, salutation, body, complimentary close) and language register (warm, first-person 'I', second-person 'You'). Students begin drafting in class; teacher circulates offering structural guidance.

Homework set: complete and polish the invitation letter draft at home. Teacher previews that learning-beyond-the-text research tasks on music therapy and the seven swaras are excellent self-study extensions for curious students.

periods.teacher_notes: The listening transcript is on p.270 of the textbook (Unit 6) — read it with distinct voices for mother and Jaspreet to make the conversation clear. For the role-play, encourage the student in the 'parent' role to show a believable shift from doubt to support during the discussion, mirroring Nabin's arc in the play. The beyond-text tasks for Section B (music therapy, swaras, poem collection, Walter de la Mare) are unanchored and may be offered as optional library research or a home project.
materials: Lined paper for invitation letter draft

items.source_lo: Student identifies and evaluates poetic devices, tone, and the poet's message by close-reading a lyric poem.
items.source_context: Bryanna T. Perkins's use of anaphora and metaphor to portray music as emotional therapy and steadfast companion.
items.item_stem:
Read the following stanzas from the poem carefully.

1. The poet uses the metaphor 'Music is the ocean / That pulls me to the shore.' Explain what this metaphor suggests about the speaker's relationship with music. Why is 'pulls' a particularly effective verb choice here?
2. How does the anaphoric repetition of 'Music is…' throughout the poem contribute to both its tone and its meaning?
3. In the final stanza, the speaker describes music as 'the needed friend / When no one seems to care.' Evaluate whether the word 'needed' adds emotional weight that a word like 'good' would not. Justify your answer.
items.visual_stimulus:
Music is the ocean
That pulls me to the shore.
Music is the rhythm
That moves me to the core.

Music is the therapy
I need when I feel blue.
Music lifts my spirits
To make sure I pull through.

The times when I'm most cheerful,
It's clear, music was there.
Music is the needed friend
When no one seems to care.

expected_elements: Q1: Explains that the ocean metaphor conveys music's irresistible, natural pull; notes 'pulls' implies involuntary attraction, not mere choice.
Q2: Identifies anaphora as creating a meditative, incantatory rhythm; explains it reinforces music's constant, all-encompassing presence across moods.
Q3: Argues 'needed' signals emotional urgency and dependency, not mere preference; contrasts with 'good' which would be casual and insufficiently intimate.

items.source_lo: Student predicts responses from context clues, then verifies and corrects predictions through active listening.
items.source_context: Mother-and-son conversation about santoor practice challenges and the solution of borrowing an instrument.
items.item_stem:
Before listening: Read the statements below and predict whether each is TRUE or FALSE based on what you might expect from a conversation between a mother and her son at a music centre. Write your prediction in the 'Prediction' column. Then listen to the conversation and confirm or correct your answers in the 'After Listening' column.

(1) Jaspreet chose the santoor as his instrument.
(2) Jaspreet's main problem is that his teacher is unavailable on weekdays.
(3) Watching online videos fully solved Jaspreet's practice difficulty.
(4) The mother's solution involves borrowing a santoor from a friend.
(5) Jaspreet asks his mother to arrange the loan immediately.

options.text: Jaspreet chose the santoor as his instrument.
Jaspreet's main problem is that his teacher is unavailable on weekdays.
Watching online videos fully solved Jaspreet's practice difficulty.
The mother's solution involves borrowing a santoor from a friend.
Jaspreet asks his mother to arrange the loan immediately.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: (1) TRUE — Jaspreet confirms he chose the santoor. (2) FALSE — the teacher comes only twice a week, not that the teacher is unavailable on weekdays specifically; the core problem is infrequent access to the teacher at the centre. (3) FALSE — Jaspreet tried videos but found them unhelpful because playing is a hands-on skill. (4) TRUE — the mother offers to borrow a santoor from a friend who used to play. (5) TRUE — Jaspreet asks her to place the request that very day.

items.source_lo: Student sustains a structured role-play, presenting and responding to arguments with logical continuity.
items.source_context: Four-way role-play debating whether music classes benefit or hinder a student's academic life.
items.item_stem:
Form a group of four. Each person takes one of the roles below. Hold a five-minute structured discussion on the motion: 'Attending regular music classes improves rather than hinders a student's academic and personal life.' Each speaker must present at least one reasoned argument and respond to at least one argument made by someone else.

Roles: (1) A student who believes music classes keep them emotionally balanced and focused. (2) A parent who worries that music practice steals time from homework. (3) A teacher who has seen music students outperform peers in concentration tasks. (4) A student who dropped music due to exam pressure and now regrets it.

expected_elements: Each participant presents a clear, role-consistent argument with at least one supporting reason.
At least one instance of direct engagement — explicitly responding to or countering another speaker's point.
Arguments are logically continuous (no abrupt topic changes mid-turn).
Language is appropriate to the register of a structured discussion, not informal chat.
Listening behaviour is evident: speakers do not merely rehearse prepared lines but adapt to what was said.

items.source_lo: Student drafts a personalised functional letter in an appropriate format and warm register.
items.source_context: A teenager inviting family members to a school event celebrating musical performance and shared heritage.
items.item_stem: Write a personal invitation letter from a student to a grandparent (or an aunt/uncle living in another city) inviting them to a school music evening called 'Strings and Roots: A Celebration of Musical Heritage'. Your letter should: be correctly formatted as a personal letter (address, date, salutation, body, closing); explain what the event will include and why you want this person to attend; share something personal about what music means to you, drawing on at least one feeling or idea from the poem 'A Friend Found in Music'; and use a warm, sincere register appropriate for a close family member.

expected_elements: Correct personal letter format: sender's address, date, appropriate salutation and closing.
Body clearly describes the event and gives a personal reason for inviting this specific family member.
At least one connection to the poem's themes — music as comfort, companion, or emotional sustenance — woven naturally into the letter.
Warm, intimate register sustained throughout; avoids formal/bureaucratic language.
Letter is cohesive: the invitation, personal reflection, and closing sentiment form a unified whole.

items.source_lo: Student identifies and uses phrasal verbs accurately, distinguishing their figurative meanings from base-verb meanings.
items.source_context: Emotion vocabulary and 'move'-based phrasal verbs drawn from a poem about music as personal sustenance.
items.item_stem:
The poem 'A Friend Found in Music' uses the phrasal verb 'pull through' and related verb phrases connected to movement and emotion. Complete the sentences below using the correct phrasal verb from the box. Then, for sentences (3) and (4), write the meaning of the phrasal verb as used in that sentence.

Phrasal verbs: pull through, move on, lift up, pull out, move to

(1) After weeks of sadness, Riya finally managed to ________ with the help of her closest friends.
(2) Listening to ragas early in the morning always seemed to ________ Nabin's mood before a performance.
(3) Despite the difficulty, Shruti refused to ________ of the fusion concert at the last moment. [Meaning: ________________]
(4) The final notes of the yazh recital seemed to ________ every listener deeply. [Meaning: ________________]

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: (1) pull through (2) lift up (3) pull out — Meaning: to withdraw from or abandon an activity one was committed to. (4) move to — Meaning: to stir strong emotion in someone; to affect someone deeply.
